items.title: India Plans to Add Strategic Fuel Reserves After Iran War Shock
items.summary: India plans to build strategic reserves of crude, liquefied petroleum gas and liquefied natural gas large enough meet as much as a month of domestic demand, as the country seeks to avoid a repeat of the supply shock created by the war in the Persian Gulf.

items.title: French Inflation Eases to Lowest Since March as Oil Prices Drop
items.summary: French inflation slowed for the first time since the Iran war broke out as energy costs fell, alleviating pressure on the European Central Bank to keep raising interest rates.

items.title: Aluminum Set for Worst Monthly Loss Since 2008 on Supply Outlook
items.summary: Aluminum headed for the steepest monthly loss since 2008 as expectations for a return of lost Middle Eastern supply saw it rapidly unwind an Iran war-induced rally.

items.title: Putin ties himself to ruling party as war fatigue bites
items.summary: United Russia is campaigning with the president for the first time in nearly 20 years

items.title: Japan’s Super-Long Bond Yields Climb as Fiscal Concerns Grow
items.summary: Japan’s super-long bond yields rose as Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s spending plans deepen concerns about fiscal expansion.

With a Fragile Ceasefire under Threat, What Future for the Strait of Hormuz?
items.summary: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran prompted Tehran to close the Strait of Hormuz, allowing only selected ships to pass through. In this Q&A, Crisis Group experts Yasmine Farouk, Chris Newton and Ali Vaez consider what could come next in this vital maritime trade corridor.

items.title: Thailand Looks to Invest in US LNG Projects to Secure Supply
items.summary: Thailand is considering investments in US liquefied natural gas export projects, according to people familiar with the matter, as the Southeast Asian importer of the fuel looks to shore up its energy security needs.

items.title: Christine Lagarde Sintra speech signals a new ECB playbook
Hormuz Traffic Picks Up as Supertankers Sail Into Persian Gulf
items.summary: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz picked up for the first time since Iran’s recent attacks on ships in the waterway, with more operators sending crude tankers into the Persian Gulf.

items.title: Growing Oil Glut Spurs Asian Refiners to Offer Cargoes to the US
items.summary: Oil output from Persian Gulf nations is ramping up so quickly as the Strait of Hormuz reopens that Asian refiners, already adequately supplied, are pushing some crude to destinations as far afield as California.

RT by @elonmusk: Being at the launch of Cybercab two years ago was magic. A decade from now there will be millions of these things rolling around the world. The war over autonomous vehicles is on, as everyone in San Francisco knows. But if you add up everyone who has been in ANY autonomous vehicle, whether from Waymo, any of the Chinese, the NVIDIA, any Tesla, it is only a few million people out of eight billion. When I first saw autonomous vehicles at Stanford University about 20 years ago they were so crappy I thought I'd never be alive to see them. I have the first video of the first Waymo on the freeway, and I've been blessed to have been able to watch the development of these up close (got the first ride in Mercedes' AI car, for instance). So many engineers spent so many hours developing them, many going down bad paths. Which happens with innovation. They will save millions of lives. Ford's head of safety told me that what kills people in a head-on wreck is the steering wheel. Now we can see a path to getting rid of the steering wheel totally. Congrats to Tesla and @elonmusk for getting here. Still a lot of work to go and many fights with many communities who will try to ban these to protect jobs. I'm very bullish on Cybercab and will buy one if sold to private citizens, as expected. Why? I never touch the steering wheel in my vehicles already and this will be the lowest cost vehicle with the most safety. And a great big screen and a great sound system to watch whatever I want. When I met the engineers at the launch they said they made many decisions to make it quieter inside, so we can do things like listen to music, video calls, or watch the World Cup. This vehicle is the iPhone moment of autonomous vehicles. One I've been lucky enough to survive the 20 years it took to see.
items.summary:
Being at the launch of Cybercab two years ago was magic.

A decade from now there will be millions of these things rolling around the world. 

The war over autonomous vehicles is on, as everyone in San Francisco knows. 

But if you add up everyone who has been in ANY autonomous vehicle, whether from Waymo, any of the Chinese, the NVIDIA, any Tesla, it is only a few million people out of eight bill

EU and NATO Members Grapple with War in Iran
items.summary: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Crisis Group’s head of EU Affairs, Lisa Musiol, about how the EU, its members and European members of NATO have responded to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran and what it reveals about EU foreign policy and its constraints.
